retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Pathway -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: All the extra-ocular muscles receive supply from the oculomotor nerve except the lateral rectus, supplied by the abducens nerve, and the superior oblique, supplied by the trochlear nerve. The oculomotor nerve also innervates levator palpebrae superioris. The lateral rectus helps in the abduction of the eyeball. The medial rectus is responsible for the adduction of the eyeball. The superior oblique and superior rectus primarily help in the intorsion of the eyeball. Inferior oblique and inferior rectus cause extortion of the eyeball. Levator palpebrae superioris elevates the eyelids.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The extraocular muscles are innervated by nerves that enter the orbit through the superior orbital fissure. The oculomotor nerve (CN III) divides into superior and inferior branches and innervates the superior, medial, and inferior recti, the levator palpebrae superioris, and the inferior oblique. It also carries presynaptic parasympathetic fibers to the ciliary ganglion. Sympathetic fibers of CN III contribute to upper eyelid retraction by innervation of the superior tarsal muscle (Müller's muscle). The trochlear nerve (CN IV) innervates the superior oblique, and the lateral rectus receives innervation from the abducens nerve (CN VI). Innervation to the orbicularis oculi is by the temporal and zygomatic branches of the facial nerve (CN VII).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Nerves -- Structure and Function -- Oculomotor Nerve (CN III)
retrieved_docs.content: The oculomotor nerve is responsible for innervating the major of the extraocular muscles of the eyes. The oculomotor nerve provides motor innervation to the superior rectus muscle, medial rectus muscle, inferior rectus muscle, inferior oblique muscle, levator palpebrae superioris muscle, ciliary muscle, and the sphincter muscle. The action of the superior rectus muscle is to rotate the eye superiorly. The medial rectus muscle functions to adduct the eye. The inferior rectus muscle rotates the eye inferiorly when it contracts. The inferior oblique muscle's role is unique because it causes torques the eye downward and laterally when it contracts. The levator palpebrae superioris muscle is responsible for elevating the eyelid during eye-opening. The iris sphincter muscle is part of the autonomic nervous system, but specifically the parasympathetic nervous system. The action of the iris sphincter muscle is to constrict the pupils (miosis). Lastly, the contraction of the ciliary muscle allows for the accommodation of the eyes. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Extraocular Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Cranial nerve III is divided into upper and lower divisions, with the upper division innervating the superior rectus and the levator palpebrae superioris and the lower division innervating the medial rectus, inferior rectus, and inferior oblique. The superior oblique is innervated by cranial nerve IV (trochlear), while the lateral rectus is innervated by cranial nerve VI (abducens). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Optic Canal -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: There are seven extraocular muscles within the ocular orbit [12] : Levator palpebrae superioris and superior tarsal muscle: levator palpebrae superioris innervated by the oculomotor nerve; superior tarsal muscle innervated by the sympathetic nervous system; both elevate the upper eyelid Superior rectus: innervated by the oculomotor nerve; the primary function is the elevation of the eyeball. Inferior rectus: innervated by the oculomotor nerve; the primary function is depression of the eyeball. Medial rectus: innervated by the oculomotor nerve; the primary function is adduction of the eyeball. Lateral rectus: innervated by the abducens nerve; the primary function is the abduction of the eyeball. Superior oblique: innervated by the trochlear nerve; the primary function is depression, abduction, and medial rotation of the eyeball. Inferior oblique: innervated by the oculomotor nerve; the primary function is elevation, abduction, and lateral rotation of the eyeball.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Nerves -- Muscles -- Oculomotor Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: Superior rectus muscle Inferior rectus muscle Medial rectus muscle Inferior oblique muscle Levator palpebrae superioris muscle Sphincter pupillae muscle Ciliary muscle

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Extraocular Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Each rectus and oblique muscle has a functional insertion point at the closest point where the muscle first contacts the globe. This point forms a tangential line from the globe to the muscle origin, known as the arc of contact. The common site of origin for the rectus muscles in the Annulus of Zinn is located at the orbital apex. The levator palpebrae superioris does not contact the globe directly but elevates the eyelid. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Medial Rectus Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Extraocular muscles have a large ratio of nerve fibers to skeletal muscle fibers—1:3 to 1:5—whereas other skeletal muscles have 1:50 to 1:125. Extraocular muscles are specialized skeletal muscles, having many fiber types like slow, fatigue-resistant tonic types and rapid, saccadic types.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Superior Oblique Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The superior oblique muscle is made up of similar muscle types to the remaining extraocular muscles, a particular type of skeletal muscle which contains a combination of type I, slow fibers, and type II, rapid fibers. Also, extraocular muscles, including the superior oblique, possess a higher ratio of nerve fibers to skeletal muscle fibers when compared to other skeletal muscles. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Inferior Oblique Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Extraocular muscles (EOMs) are crucial for ocular movement. The superior and inferior obliques and the 4 recti work together to move the eyes in various directions (see Image . Oculomotor Nerve Pathway). The inferior oblique is almost entirely muscular, unlike the superior oblique, which is both muscular and tendinous. The inferior oblique is primarily responsible for the eye's external rotation. The muscle's secondary and tertiary actions are elevation and abduction, respectively. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cranial Nerve III Palsy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The third cranial nerve is also known as oculomotor nerve and has 2 major components: Outer parasympathetic fibers that supply the ciliary muscles and the sphincter pupillae Inner somatic fibers that supply the levator palpebrae superioris in the eyelid (which retracts the upper eyelid) and the 4 extraocular muscles (superior, middle, inferior recti, and inferior oblique). LR6(SO4)3 is a simple mnemonic representing the innervation of the extraocular muscles. It stands for: LR6: Lateral rectus muscle which is supplied by the sixth cranial nerve SO4: Superior oblique muscle which is supplied by the fourth cranial nerve 3: The third cranial nerve supplies other extraocular muscles

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Inferior Rectus Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary action of the inferior rectus muscle is to depress the eye, causing the cornea and pupil to move inferiorly. The inferior rectus originates from the annulus of Zinn and courses anteriorly and laterally along the orbital floor, making an angle of 23 degrees with the visual axis. This angle causes the secondary and tertiary actions of the inferior rectus muscle to be adduction and extorsion (excycloduction).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Extraocular Muscles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are 7 extraocular muscles. There are 4 rectus muscles, 2 oblique muscles, and the standalone levator palpebrae superioris. The 4 rectus muscles are the medial rectus, lateral rectus, superior rectus, and inferior rectus. The oblique muscles are the superior and inferior obliques. The levator palpebrae superioris is primarily responsible for eyelid elevation (see Image. The Extraocular Muscles of the Orbit). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Orbit -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The levator palpebrae superioris muscle, which receives nerve supply from CN III, elevates the upper eyelid. It is superior to the superior rectus muscle at the roof of the orbit, and these two muscles join in a common aponeurosis anteriorly. This intimate muscular relationship explains why the eye elevates as the upper eyelid is retracted. The extraocular muscles and their actions are as follows: [2] Superior rectus: Elevates, adducts and rotates medially Medial rectus: Adducts Inferior rectus: Depresses, adducts and rotates laterally Lateral rectus: Abducts Superior oblique: Depresses, abducts, and rotates medially Inferior oblique: Elevates, abducts, and rotates laterally

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve -- Structure and Function -- Cranial Nerves III, IV, and VI (Oculomotor, Trochlear, and Abducens Nerves)
retrieved_docs.content: Cranial nerve III innervates most of the eye muscles by splitting into superior and inferior branches, which innervate the remaining 3 rectus muscles, the inferior oblique, and the skeletal muscle component of the levator palpebrae superioris. Besides its GSE function, cranial nerve III also contains a general visceral efferent (GVE) component originating from the Edinger-Westphal nucleus, also known as the accessory or visceral oculomotor nucleus. These parasympathetic fibers travel with the oculomotor nerve to synapse in the ciliary ganglion within the orbit.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Muscles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The muscles of the eye are integral to its function and motion. Muscles directly associated with the eye include the extraocular muscles which control the external movement of the eye; the intraocular muscles, which are responsible for pupil accommodation and reaction to light; and the protractor and retractors of the eyelids. Deficits in the muscles or the nerves innervating these muscles can result in functional impairment of the involved structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it involves nerve findings, and muscular atrophy/Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), which reflect the nerves’ integral role in motor and sensory function. Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is recognized as having a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, indicating that it functions as a neurotransmitter within the nervous system. This classification underscores acetylcholine’s role in transmitting signals between nerve cells and across neuromuscular junctions, which is fundamental to processes such as muscle contraction, autonomic nervous system regulation, and cognitive functions. Understanding acetylcholine’s position as a neurotransmitter is essential for comprehending its impact on both normal physiological signaling and various clinical conditions where its pathways may be altered.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: response patterns (“مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي”), and combined myasthenic‑Bell’s palsy presentations. The qualifier “Neurologic” links many of these concepts to a neurologic domain, while the child qualifier “paralysis” (شلل) underscores motor loss as a frequent clinical consequence of peripheral‑nerve pathology. “Nervous system and sense organ diseases” functions as an intermediate class that bridges the overarching “Other disorders of nervous system” with more specific sensory, ocular and headache conditions. Its children comprise unspecified eye and adnexa disease (“Aandoening van oog en adnexen, niet gespecificeerd”), eye disorder (“اضطراب العين”), and the headache spectrum including migraine. This class is also associated with the Arabic concepts of pain (ألم) and paralysis (شلل), reflecting its position at the intersection of general nervous‑system pathology, pain syndromes and motor impairment. Procedural and anatomical links are explicitly recorded. Injury of peripheral nerves

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: النفق الكعبري), **phantom‑limb syndrome**, **horseshoe kidney with neurogenic bladder** (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية), and **reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb** (الحثل الانعكاسي الودي للطرف السفلي). Procedural interventions include **decompression of peripheral nerves**, **carpal tunnel release** (تخفيف ضغط النفق الرسغي), and **incision of nerve** (part of the decompression peripheral‑nerve family). **Cranial‑nerve disorders** are represented by **olfactory nerve disorder** (اضطراب العصب الشمي), **facial nerve disorder** (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), and the unspecified eye‑and‑adnexal disease **“Aandoening van oog en adnexen, niet gespecificeerd.”** **Motor and muscle disorders** include **muscle disorder** (اضطراب العضلات), **neuromuscular disease** (اعتلال عصبي عضلي), **muscular atrophy** (وهن عضلي وبيل), and the broader label **“System disorder of the nervous system.”** Rare hereditary syndromes such as **Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease** (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث) are linked to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Neuromuscular disease (اعتلال عصبي عضلي) is connected to paralysis (شلل), indicating that disorders affecting both nerves and muscles can result in loss of motor function. Muscle disorder (اضطراب العضلات) also has a relationship with paralysis, reinforcing the link between impaired muscle function and the development of paralysis. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are related to muscle disorder, suggesting that pathological changes in joint‑related structures can influence muscle health and thereby contribute to paralytic outcomes. More specific conditions, such as lesion in the sciatic nerve (آفة في العصب الوركي) and facial nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), are identified as narrower relationships to paralysis, highlighting that focal nerve injuries or dysfunctions are precise causes of paralytic deficits. Together, these concepts illustrate how a spectrum of neuromuscular, muscular, and peripheral nerve pathologies are interrelated and can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of evaluating both muscle and nerve integrity in patients presenting with motor impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) has a broad relationship with neurosurgery (جراحة الأعصاب), indicating that many surgical interventions target or involve this central organ system. It also serves as the anatomical site for procedures such as muscular neuro blockade (إحصار عصبي عضلي), highlighting its role in therapeutic techniques that modulate nerve‑muscle interactions. In addition, the nervous system is the anatomic site associated with other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, linking it to a spectrum of inherited and progressive disorders. Those hereditary and degenerative conditions make use of diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور), suggesting that inflammatory processes of the brain and spinal axis are involved in their pathophysiology. Together, these connections underscore the central importance of the nervous system in surgical practice, procedural interventions, and the manifestation of certain hereditary and degenerative neurological diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: decompression procedures (تخفيف ضغط العصب المحيطي), including carpal tunnel release (تخفيف ضغط النفق الرسغي). “Inflammation of the tendon sheath” (التهاب زَلِيْلِ الوتَر) is linked to carpal tunnel syndrome, and broader disorders of the synovium, tendon and bursa are associated with “اضطراب العضلات” (muscle disorder), which can progress to paralysis (شلل). Neurologic qualifier values are attached to peripheral neuropathy, alcoholic polyneuropathy, trigeminal neuralgia pain (ألم العصب الثلاثي التوائم), Bell’s palsy (شلل بل) and carpal tunnel syndrome (متلازمة النفق الرسغي). Muscular neuropathy (اعتلال عصبي عضلي) also shares a relationship with paralysis, underscoring the functional impact of nerve disease on motor function. Collectively, this framework integrates symptom‑level concepts (pain, paralysis) with anatomical sites (peripheral nerves, specific cranial and spinal nerves, tendon sheaths, synovium), disease entities (headache, ocular disorders, the extensive list of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1078
retrieved_docs.content: Headache, including migraine, is categorized within the broader group of Nervous system and sense organ diseases, which in turn is considered a subcategory of paralysis. Within this hierarchy, eye disorder (اضطراب العين) is identified as a child condition of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. Migraine and Headache Syndromes are placed under the umbrella of neuromuscular disease (اعتلال عصبي عضلي), a condition that is directly related to paralysis. Other disorders of the synovium, tendon, and bursa are linked to muscle disorder (اضطراب العضلات), and this muscle disorder also has a direct relationship with paralysis. Specific nerve pathologies, such as sciatic nerve lesion (آفة في العصب الوركي) and facial nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), are described as narrower manifestations that connect to paralysis. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how various headache syndromes, eye and muscle disorders, and distinct nerve lesions are interconnected through a common association with neuromuscular impairment and paralysis, highlighting the importance of recognizing these links for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: pathologies, entrapment syndromes, headache and facial‑nerve disorders, musculoskeletal pathology and diagnostic imaging, thereby supporting comprehensive patient assessment, precise diagnosis and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال عصبي عضلي (muscle neuropathy) is linked to شلل (paralysis), indicating that neuropathic involvement of muscle tissue can result in loss of motor function. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are associated with اضطراب العضلات (muscle disorder), showing that pathological changes in peri‑articular structures often affect muscle integrity. اضطراب العضلات itself has a relationship with شلل, reinforcing the concept that muscle disorders can progress to or coexist with paralysis. اضطراب العصب الوجهي (facial nerve disorder) includes ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (upper facial atrophy) as a specific manifestation, and it also has a narrower relationship with شلل, suggesting that facial nerve pathology may contribute to localized or generalized motor weakness. آفة في العصب الوركي (lesion in the sciatic nerve) similarly has a narrower relationship with شلل, highlighting that focal peripheral nerve lesions can directly produce paralysis of the innervated limb. Collectively, these connections illustrate how neuromuscular and musculoskeletal disorders—ranging from muscle neuropathy and tendon‑synovial disease to facial and sciatic nerve lesions—are interrelated pathways that can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of both nerve and muscle pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Headache; including migraine is classified as a condition that belongs to the broader category of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. The category Nervous system and sense organ diseases also includes the child entity اضطراب العين (eye disorder). Recognizing that both headache—including migraine—and eye disorders are subtypes of Nervous system and sense organ diseases helps clinicians organize related symptoms, consider shared pathophysiological mechanisms, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base groups a wide array of neurologic and neuromuscular conditions under the umbrella of **Other disorders of the nervous system**, emphasizing their inter‑relationships with pain, paralysis, and sensory disturbance. Generic pain classifications (including “Pain, not elsewhere classified”) and specific pain entities such as **trigeminal nerve pain** and **facial nerve disorders** (including **facial paralysis** and upper‑facial atrophy) are linked to peripheral nerve pathology. Lesions of individual peripheral nerves—**plantar nerve**, **calcaneal (tibial) nerve**, **sciatic nerve**, **thoracic peripheral nerves**, and hemorrhage into a nerve trunk—are each associated with localized motor loss, foot deformities, or broader paralytic outcomes. **Peripheral neuropathy** is a central subcategory connected to pain and paralysis and subdivided into numerous etiologies: **alcoholic polyneuropathy**, **diabetic neuropathy**, **idiopathic progressive neuropathy**,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: strategies in the management of neuromuscular, neuro‑vascular and entrapment neuropathies, supporting precise diagnosis, coding and coordinated patient care across overlapping neurologic, sensory and musculoskeletal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: tendon‑sheath inflammation (التهاب زَلِيْلِ الوتَر). Procedural concepts include incision of a nerve and peripheral‑nerve decompression (تخفيف ضغط العصب المحيطي), with child procedures such as wrist‑tunnel (carpal tunnel) decompression (تخفيف ضغط النفق الرسغي). Decompression of the peripheral nerve is directly linked to tarsal tunnel syndrome, and lesions of the tibial nerve (آفة في العصب الكعبري), sciatic nerve (آفة في العصب الوركي), and facial nerve (اضطراب العصب الوجهي) each have narrower relationships to paralysis (شلل). Carpal tunnel decompression is related to tarsal tunnel syndrome, illustrating that pressure‑relief strategies in one anatomical tunnel inform management of analogous compressive neuropathies elsewhere. Cranial nerve disorders include facial nerve lesion, Bell’s palsy, combined myasthenic‑Bell’s palsy, and sciatic nerve lesion, all associated with motor weakness or paralysis.
